questions.q: In a Kubernetes HA control plane, how do multiple kube-controller-manager instances coordinate to avoid conflicting actions?
options: All instances run actively and merge their results
They use leader election — only one instance is active at a time, others are on standby
Each instance manages a separate subset of namespaces
The Load Balancer forwards requests to only one controller-manager
questions.explanation: kube-controller-manager (and kube-scheduler) use a built-in leader election mechanism backed by a Lease object in the kube-system namespace. Only the elected leader actively reconciles cluster state; the others remain on standby and take over only if the leader fails.

questions.q: For a small team running development workloads on-premises without requiring high availability, which cluster setup is most appropriate?
options: kubeadm HA with 3 master nodes and 5 worker nodes
k3s or kubeadm with a 3-node cluster (1 master, 2 workers)
A stacked etcd cluster with 7 nodes
questions.explanation: For small dev teams without HA requirements, k3s (lightweight) or a simple kubeadm 3-node setup is the right balance of simplicity and functionality. Managed clouds (EKS) and 3+ master HA setups are overkill for non-production workloads.

questions.q: What is the recommended minimum CPU and RAM for an etcd node in a production Kubernetes cluster?
options: 1 CPU, 2 GB RAM on any disk type
2 CPU, 8 GB RAM on SSD-backed, low-latency storage
4 CPU, 4 GB RAM on HDD storage
8 CPU, 16 GB RAM with network-attached storage
questions.explanation: etcd is I/O sensitive. The official recommendation is 2 CPU and 8 GB RAM minimum, with SSD-backed storage and low-latency networking. High write latency (from slow disks or network) directly causes etcd heartbeat timeouts and leader re-elections.
